steps.title: Use Text 2's gateway idea
steps.content: Text 2 says London represents capitalism, pragmatism, and material wealth, while the countryside represents idealism, tradition, authentic connection, aesthetic value, and the infinite. King's Cross matters because it is the route from London toward that countryside, so Text 2 treats it as a point of access to the novel's idealist values.

steps.title: Read Text 1 through that lens
steps.content: In Text 1, Margaret sees King's Cross as suggesting infinity, not ordinary prosperity. The station sits behind St. Pancras and seems to comment on materialism. That description fits Text 2's distinction between material London and the more intangible values associated with the countryside.

steps.title: Reject materialist readings
steps.content: Choice A reverses the direction by making King's Cross bring materialism into rural spaces. Choice B invents Margaret's disillusionment with authentic connection. Choice D treats King's Cross as evidence of capitalist ascendancy. Choice C presents King's Cross as a connection to intangible abundance associated with rural spaces. The answer is C.
